review_text: NAFA Arts Preschool has a very well-rounded and holistic curriculum for their students. My daughter has learned and grown a lot since she joined in January 2023. The Principal and Teachers are very caring, encouraging, and supportive. Aside from the great curriculum the school offers, they also teach and instill good lifestyle / hygiene habits to the young ones (which I personally really love and appreciate). The meals served in school are always nutritious and sufficient. I can focus on my work knowing my daughter is in excellent hands everyday.

As a parent whose child has experienced 2 different preschools (and with nephews in other preschools), I can confidently say that APS has exceeded all my expectations. Not only do the teachers genuinely care about the well-being and development of each child, the dedication and compassion to each child has deeply touched our hearts.

The focus on arts education/exposure plays a vital role in fostering creativity and imagination - we are inspired by the various forms of art he is exposed to in school. Regular updates on his progress, together with weekly insights on his class activities have made us feel involved and assured about his development.

We definitely recommend NAFA Arts Preschool to any parent seeking a nurturing and stimulating environment for their child!

The principal of NAFA Art Pre School does not CARE ABOUT HER STUDENTS AND STAFF, and I must say, it was one of the worst experiences I've had with an educational administrator. This principal was not only uncaring, but also irresponsible in her duties.

From the moment I approached them with a concern about my child's academic progress DUE TO HIGH TURNOVER RATES OF TEACHERS LEAVING, it was clear that the principal had little interest in helping. They seemed dismissive of my concerns and did not provide any concrete solutions or support. In fact, they seemed to go out of their way to avoid dealing with the issue, which only made me more frustrated and upset.

It was as if they were content to let things slide and avoid any confrontation, regardless of the potential consequences for the students involved.

Overall, I would not recommend this principal to anyone seeking a responsible and caring leader for their child's education. Their lack of concern and irresponsible behavior have left a lasting negative impression on me, and I can only imagine how their actions have affected other parents and students in the school.

place_name: Viv's Schoolhouse - Best International Preschool In Singapore
review_text:
We found out about Viv’s Schoolhouse through the recommendation of a friend and after going for the school tour, we were impressed with what the school offered in terms of curriculum and school activities such as their monthly excursions, cookery, water play and outdoor time.

Our boys (2 & 5) have been with the school for over 6 months now and the only regret I have is that I didn’t send my kids in earlier! Especially my older son, who used to be disinterested and unchallenged in his previous preschool, is now thriving in this school. He looks forward to the many daily activities which his teachers prepared to make lessons fun and engaging for them to learn through play. I’ve also seen a huge improvement in his reading and writing skills.

Photos of my kids going on excursions, learning and having fun in school are always updated very quickly and frequently in the school app. Any concerns about my kids are also addressed promptly. The school also stresses on primary school readiness and inculcating good values in the children.

I highly recommend Viv’s schoolhouse to all parents who wish for their kids to have a great preschool experience where they’ll be supported by a great team of educators and a quality curriculum.

place_name: Viv's Schoolhouse - Best International Preschool In Singapore
review_text:
⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️
We are truly grateful to Viv School House for preparing our child, Tiffany so well for Primary 1. The teachers are caring, patient, and very dedicated, and the curriculum is thoughtfully planned to build confidence and strong foundations.

What really impressed us is how aligned their teaching is with primary school expectations. We were happy to learn that P1 will include Show & Tell for English lesson and Pinyin for Mandarin lesson. Viv has already been exposing the children to these concepts, making the transition much smoother and less stressful.

My child has grown not only academically but also in confidence and communication skills. Thank you Viv School House for creating such a supportive and nurturing environment. Highly recommended to parents looking for a preschool that truly prepares children for the next stage of learning! 💕

place_name: Viv's Schoolhouse - Best International Preschool In Singapore
review_text:
Our daughter attended Viv's and she had a blast. The school's curriculum is very holistic and it is one of the reasons we were drawn to it. They are one of the few pre schools that have their own dedicated curriculum specialist. Teachers here are warm and lovely, we felt at ease as parents with the constant updates and the way our daughter interacted with the teachers.

There are too many things to highlight but the monthly excursions, Japanese classes, and swimming classes are something you can look forward to that are not that found in other schools.

Thank you for being a great institution for our family!

place_name: Viv's Schoolhouse - Best International Preschool In Singapore
review_text:
After experiencing three different preschools, including an international one overseas, I can confidently say that Viv’s Schoolhouse has been the most suitable for my son. What sets this school apart is the perfect balance between play and academics—ensuring that children learn in a way that feels natural and enjoyable rather than forced.

The teachers are truly dedicated, patient, and nurturing, creating a warm and supportive environment where kids feel safe and encouraged to explore. It’s evident that they genuinely care about the children’s growth, both academically and emotionally.

Additionally, the school management team has put in a lot of heart and effort into shaping the school into a space that prioritizes young children’s development. From the thoughtfully designed curriculum to the engaging activities (cookery class, speech and drama, monthly excursions etc), everything reflects a deep understanding of what young learners need.

Seeing my child excited to go to school everyday and thriving in this environment has been incredibly reassuring. I highly recommend Viv’s Schoolhouse to any parent looking for a preschool that truly understands and caters to young children’s learning and well-being.

place_name: Viv's Schoolhouse - Best International Preschool In Singapore
review_text: Viv’s Schoolhouse offers educational programmes focused on early childhood development. The school has modern facilities that support a nurturing environment for children’s learning and growth.

Been a very pleasant experience with LSH Kent Vale. The teachers are amazing and caring. The school management also provides regular updates so quite pleased with the communications. There are both structured and unstructured programs provided. My two kids look forward to school every day. The playground is within the school compound. There is also another playground opposite the school so it can be after school hangout place.

Both of my sons attended this childcare, and I couldn't be more pleased with the experience. The school provides a warm and nurturing environment where children feel safe and supported from day one. The teachers are incredibly caring, attentive, and genuinely invested in each child’s development.

What really stood out to us was how well-structured the program is — there’s a thoughtful balance of learning, play, and rest that kept our boys engaged and happy throughout the day. We noticed real growth in their confidence, social skills, and early learning milestones.

It's clear that this childcare center prioritizes the well-being and development of every child. We’re truly grateful for the care and commitment shown to our family. Highly recommended!

It’s clear that the teachers truly care about the children. They take the time to understand each child’s unique qualities and nurture their individual potential — something genuinely special. The kindergarten is well-organized, with a wide range of enriching activities and excellent facilities. The warm, safe environment makes it a wonderful place for children to learn, grow, and flourish.

Couldn't have been happier to enrol my son at LSH Kent Vale for the past 2.5 years. The school has a great curriculum, enrichment activities eg sport ball, speech & drama and an exclusive playground. When we were seeing a speech therapist due to a slight speech delay, the teachers and management were very accommodating to our requests such as sending videos of him eating for the therapist to review. I also like that the school tries to engage the children in sensory and outdoor play. Most importantly, each teacher is very caring and goes beyond their class to interact with other children. A big shoutout to Teacher Annisa, Wu Laoshi and Cong Laoshi for being ever so patient and attentive to my son's needs!

On the surface, it's a cheap and cheerful pre-school. But there are significant downsides. First, there is a set curriculum and it advances at a set pace. Students who do not keep up will simply be left behind. If you don't ask the right questions during the student-teacher meeting, they won't even mention that your child has fallen behind. They simply expect that some students will; it's not treated as a problem. Second, when you ask whether the teacher can give your child work that is adapted to their level, they (politely) refuse. As my child's K2 teacher told me, their way of "adapting" to the child is to lower their expectations for what your child can produce. 4 and 5 year olds are already being marked as less-than and no efforts are made to catch them up to their peers. Third, the class sizes for K1 and K2 are huge (up to 30 students per room). Overall, the place is run like a factory.

School of the Arts (SOTA) is a truly unique institution that offers a vibrant and nurturing environment for young creatives. It’s rare to find a place that balances high-level academic rigor with such a deep, specialized focus on the arts.

​What Makes it Stand Out:
-​Creative Atmosphere: From the moment you walk in, the energy is palpable. The campus architecture itself is inspiring, providing the perfect backdrop for students to explore their crafts—whether it’s dance, music, theater, visual arts, or literary arts.

-​Dedicated Faculty: The educators here are more than just teachers; they are mentors who are clearly passionate about both their artistic disciplines and the growth of their students.

-​Holistic Development: SOTA doesn't just produce great artists; it fosters critical thinkers. The integration of the IB curriculum ensures that students are well-prepared for any path they choose after graduation.

I went for the open house and it was absolutely amazing. The campus is huge and its architecture is just beyond beautiful. The students are so kind and full of energy! The activities were engaging and interesting. I went for a tour around the campus and explored about what the students did in the school. I love it:3.

SOTA building @ Orchard Road showcases a very artistic and modern architectural design that is truly eye-catching.
Its unique facade and creative structure make it stand out beautifully along the bustling Orchard Road, reflecting its role as a centre for the arts.

Know nothing about the school but what an incredibly beautiful building. Happy that the building seems to be fully open again after couple years of covid restrictions.

Loitered to the public gallery on 2nd floor where some of student’s works are on display.  Mostly photography and cartoon style drawing. Very quiet on a weekend afternoon.

Great place for Dram, music and such activities and graduation.

Building architecture is excellent

After 15 years, NJC's boarding school (only) was officially closed in 2024 to return the land for state use.

These images hope to honour the memories of those 500 students and teachers boarded there every year

For more at #shuutur

Very disappointing. The secondary level teachers dont really teach. I was expecting some principles to be taught in class so I kept quiet and observed my kid's progress. Wasn't taught. Disappointed. They also got 1 alumni to teach while waiting for her A results. Teachers not teaching in way that can be understood. The school relies on private tuition and hard self study. A few teachers talks disrespectful like are sarcastic. Student prefers the previous principal

One of the famous schools in Singapore and undoubtedly with lots of heritage, history and culture. As you step into the school you feel the nostalgia and history working together to build a wonderful, hopeful and resilient new generation one after the other.

Shoes and shoelaces that are garish in nature are not allowed. W school, W people, W teachers, and most importantly, W chillies, popcorn and math.

Everyday I go to school and I look forward to seeing my math work. The piles and piles of math homework does not do itself. It sure is great experience and practice!!!

The accidental cohesive nature of this school is truly great. All that gleams is not gold. The equal funding given to the aesthetics and science departments are truly interesting.

I recall the days I went to NJC. Oh my, it has been quite a bit... I can recall the days we got our exam results back. I remember the smiles our teacher tried to hide from the students when they gave us those earth-shatteringly wonderful results. They smiled so wide, so wide we could barely see the tears forming in their eyes as they looked at our shattered expressions in pride.

The teachers love teaching, sometimes a little too much. I see their beautiful teary-eyes smiles upon their weary faces whenever they step into class. I see it, I feel it, I know it, it is ingrained into each and every one of their fabric of reality. As an alumni of this school, I missed the days of boarding. The chaotic thwacking of doors whenever we closed a door was truly inspiring.

The myths are not true. We do not have a leaky roof. In fact, it is the best roofs of all. The roof's whole is about winning. In fact, it is so winnable that it has always allowed water through it. It has never lost its purpose in letting water through.

One of the key problems today is that math is undervalued in this society, and that there is too much emphasis on the pursuit of arts. What even is the use for arts? Useless pining? Useless uselessness? Useless emotions? None of these things are needed. Join NJC, for you will see the world for what it is, through good, or through better, or through best, and that, is through math.

Our integrated programme is good. No, the best at INTEGRATING!!!! Our streak of streaky teary students crying tears of joy at the sight of their beautiful eggs received on their math papers is unmatched. One must join this institution to truly understand the meaning of life. For it is to MATH.

I truly apologise for this accidental cohesive multicultural heritage-ridden serviced and honoured post. NJCians truly deserve more math homework.

Teachers are unable to teach. You need to engage tutors to get your child to understand the material. They overload the students with homework and you pray that your child survives this barrage of tests, the humiliation of failing and the lack of proper help to improve. Don't send your child here unless he/she is prepared to weather the storm and you are prepared for the many teacher/parent sessions.
Attended ACJC's traditional biennial funfair. Such an exciting event! Kudos to all the students and teachers who made the event so grand.

Not only does this school love sucking out money from its students to allegedly maintain facilities that 1. students NEVER get to use and 2. NEVER become cleaner or better in condition, they also enjoy developing bladder diseases in their poor students.

Teachers, especially the discipline master, ridicule and punish, sometimes even forbid, students from simply using the toilet. A 5 minute trip to the toilet to empty one’s bladder from holding it in for hours is seen as an act of sin, a crime being committed by this school that praises itself for being so loving with the grace of God.

During chapel, assembly, or school events where students are gathered in the hall, using to the toilet is seen as a threat, a crime. Students will either be held captive in another room where they will not even be able to see what is going on in the main hall to appreciate the event, will be told to take the route that is much longer to come back to the hall (which makes absolutely no sense as one would have to walk past much more people if their reason is disturbing the course of event), or simply, told no and scolded for “not being able to hold it in”.

Teachers say to students in a humiliating, condescending tone that if they cannot hold their bladder for more than a few hours, they should “get it checked”. This is either implying that students should not stay hydrated or using the toilet is undesirable, when it is part and parcel of how the normal human body works. Over time, with students not being able to use the restroom when they urgently need it, kidney and bladder diseases may be manifested. Urinary Tract Infections are amongst one of the many diseases students would have a much higher chance of having after holding in their urine for extended periods of time just to adhere to some school expectations. Not only that, it is simply extremely uncomfortable sitting still with a full bladder.

I sincerely hope the school will be more considerate of students’ health and needs and be more understanding.

I went to ACJC this morning at 11:30am as I wanted to purchase a swimming paddle for my child. At the entrance, the security guard Mr. Liao asked me to register and requested for my IC. I simply asked him whether it was necessary to provide my IC, but instead of explaining politely, he responded in a rude manner and told me not to “talk too much,” threatening not to let me in and even challenging me to complain to the authorities.

I believe such an attitude is not appropriate, especially within a school environment that should set an example for students in communication and respect. If students were to overhear such an exchange, what impression would they be left with?

I sincerely hope the school can look into this matter and ensure better communication and professionalism from the staff. Your improvement will be very much appreciated.

Wonderful Junior College providing a holistic education. Tremendously benefitted from my experience here at ACJC in 2003-2004, with lifelong friendships and bonds still going strong 20+ years later. Blessed to be part of the ACSian family, with teachers and even our former Principal being ever supportive of our individual endeavours. The sports facilities were fantastic too from the track to the swimming pool and gym, I credit a lot of my sporting journey from this too. Thank you, ACJC! 🙏🏻

The location is strategic, as it sits between 2 mrt stations, and surrounded by many eateries and shopping malls. The sport complex is big, there's football field, running track, swimming pools, as well as badminton court.

Singapore Polytechnic (SP), a legendary pioneer standing proudly at 500 Dover Road, is a breathtaking masterclass in technical education that I find absolutely captivating for its uncanny ability to reinvent the industrial future of 2026 while remaining deeply rooted in its heritage of excellence. Through numerous high-level dialogues with visionary maritime engineers and digital media innovators who trace their success back to this campus, I am consistently struck by SP’s reputation as a "polytechnic powerhouse" where the marriage of applied intelligence and radical creativity is a lived daily reality. Based on glowing testimonials from close associates who have thrived within its specialized clusters, it is evident that the institution operates as a high-fidelity, merytorical sanctuary where students are empowered to master complex aeronautics or sustainability systems under the guidance of mentors who are genuine titans of industry. Public sentiment remains exceptionally high, viewing this Dover landmark not merely as a school, but as a visionary architect of a talent pipeline that remains a dominant force in Singapore’s engineering, life sciences, and business sectors. Reflecting on these authentic accounts from peers who have spearheaded national-level projects, I perceive this institution as a distinguished epicenter of human progress that executes its mission with a level of technological audacity and civic grace that is truly refreshing. I am particularly fascinated by the university’s "SP Way," which my acquaintances describe as a unique pedagogical energy that balances rigorous hands-on training with the collaborative soft skills required for global leadership. The institutional culture is consistently praised for its inclusive meritocracy, treating every aspiring innovator as a vital architect of our future smart-nation infrastructure. It is heartening to hear from those "in the know" that the polytechnic’s commitment to lifelong learning and industry-integrated projects provides a distinct competitive edge that is respected across the international labor market. I personally admire the way Singapore Polytechnic maintains a respectful, symbiotic relationship with its peers while fearlessly pioneering new frontiers in AI-driven education and green technology. Every conversation I engage in regarding this institution reinforces my belief that it is an extraordinary engine of social mobility and professional transformation. Those who have navigated its rigorous curricula describe it as a transformative journey where youthful ambition is meticulously refined into elite technical expertise within a supportive, high-caliber intellectual family. Ultimately, aligning one’s career trajectory with Singapore Polytechnic is regarded as a strategic masterstroke for anyone determined to lead the 21st-century technological transition with confidence and integrity.
Amazing! It is great place for the student. I visited for a mentoring session and it was a great experience.

Singapore Polytechnic honestly feels like its own ecosystem. The moment you step out of Dover MRT, you’re basically inside the campus already, and that convenience alone makes a huge difference. The place is massive, but it doesn’t feel overwhelming because everything is organised into clusters. Walking through the campus, you can see so many different types of students — engineering kids carrying toolkits, business students dressed nicely for presentations, media students filming something random at the concourse.

The school has a very “industry-ready” vibe. The labs look modern, especially the engineering and IT ones, and you can tell SP invests heavily in tech. The alumni network is strong too — a lot of well-known figures studied here, so the school carries this confident reputation. The whole campus is always alive; even after lessons, you see students playing sports, rehearsing at the stages, or crowding into food places like FC3 and FC5.

What I liked:

Super clean and modern environment — nothing feels rundown.

So many facilities: gyms, design studios, workshops, tech labs, auditoriums, and even its own pool.

Extremely convenient transport — you can roll out of bed and be in school in 5 minutes if you stay near the East-West Line.

The culture feels mature, almost like mini-university life.

What could be better:

Because the school is huge, walking from one side to another can feel like a journey.

Popular food places get packed extremely fast during lunch.

Some of the older blocks are showing age, but still in good condition.

Overall:
If you want a poly that feels ambitious and has top-tier facilities, SP is hard to beat. It feels like a place where people genuinely get things done.

Easy place to access as its near Dover mrt. May need a student pass to get thru the gantry. I was there to attend a public workshop and so the helpful security assisted me. Many students I met to ask for directions were very helpful. There are a few Food Courts (air-conditioning and non air-conditioning) plus varieties of food at reasonable price. As the venue is huge, one need to walk (take it as part of exercise). O
Present for graduation day a well known polytechnic in Singapore

Nice seats
Cold AC
Nice auditorium. Comfortable seat. Was there attending a graduation ceremony. Aircon not too cold but I didn't check if the seat had a pull out table.
Can hold more than 800 people all at once. Good location, just beside Dover MRT.

Very good! When I stepped into the campus, I felt the youthful atmosphere. The activities on the open day were rich in content, and each link was arranged in an orderly manner. Both teachers and students were very enthusiastic and professional. The signboards were also very clear. If I was not sure about something, there would always be enthusiastic students to guide me.
Back here again for my another kid's grad.

The University of the Arts Singapore (UAS) is centrally located at the National Design Centre near Bras Basah and Bugis MRT, Singapore’s arts, heritage, and design precinct. UAS is Singapore’s first arts university, founded on an alliance of Singapore’s foremost arts institutions, LASALLE College of the Arts (LASALLE) and Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts (NAFA).
I don't know about the school itself, but their lemon cake at the Café is delicious♥️‼️🎂
UAS has helped me to achieve my dream career in the arts

Very rich collection of artifacts, some are even thousand year old!! Also have modern arts & sculptures, the history of boats section was also sooo nice!! And the museum is free to enter.
Met two very nice person at the reception, they are generous, caring & friendly 👍 Overall, My experience was very good. Must visit if you have time 💚

I recently visited the NUS Museum and was absolutely fascinated by its collection focused on animals and natural history. One of the highlights was seeing the impressive dinosaur bones and massive whale skeleton, which offered a real sense of scale and history that photos simply can’t capture.

The museum features countless specimens that showcase the incredible diversity of the animal kingdom. From preserved insects and reptiles to birds and marine creatures, each display taught me something new about how different species evolved and adapted to their environments. It was amazing to learn how animals are all interconnected and how their evolutionary paths reflect broader changes in the world around them.

The exhibits do a fantastic job explaining complex topics like natural selection, extinction, and ecological balance in an engaging and easy-to-understand way. I left with a deeper appreciation of the delicate harmony in nature and how humans fit into the story of evolution.

I highly recommend booking tickets in advance to have enough time to explore every section without rushing. Overall, it was an eye-opening and enriching visit — perfect for anyone curious about how life on Earth has developed and continues to change over millions of years.

It's free and it's just opposite of Natural history museum.
We can be sure to find Ancient Chinese pots, earthenware, ceramics. There are few artifacts from India and one from combodia.
There are some paintings and art installation. A few crazy things were there in sculptures and art.
Otherwise simple, neat museum which you can cover in a couple of hours

Entry to the museum is free but depending on the crowd outside you could be waiting up to 30 minutes to get in. Upon entering, the staff will give you a briefing on what you can and cannot do inside of the building. There are three levels. The first and third floors being dedicated to pottery of different time periods. The second floor is more for temporary exhibits and more exploratory things. The exhibits on this floor could use more explanation instead of a number next to such exhibits, as I had a hard time understanding why it was even displayed in the beginning. Overall a good place to visit if you happen to be within a stones throw from here.

Free admission. Clean and well maintained.
Enjoy the peace and quiet here while admiring the art pieces.

Excellent and all-rounded tuition centre. Provides tuition of a variety of subjects with highly dedicated and amazing teachers! They are extremely helpful and do their best to help you whatever problem you have. The teachers’ teaching style is unique and they can adjust the lessons to the topics that you are facing difficulty in.

I go to tuition here for Math lessons. Ms Yvonne Chen is very helpful and patient with her students and always stops to ensure everyone understands the content fully before continuing. Under her guidance and help my math results have steadily improved and i strongly recommend anyone looking for tuition to attend Future Academy.
My tutor is nice caring and supportive and she’s a very good teacher and help brush up my math. She goes the extra mile in guiding me. Highly recommended.

I attended class here when my GPA for math was very bad. In Y2 I managed to get a 4.0 after one year of lessons! Very grateful for the entertaining and enriching lessons. There is also good use of digital tools to enhance learning, even if one cannot make it physically, they are able to attend the lesson online in a way that is equally educational.

It is amazing, the teachers are great there and I really enjoyed it. I really recommend it because it helped me with my math a lot and it is a very good place to learn. I think it was very good that I chose to go here because it was so worth it. I felt very safe and I could ask any question, and they would help explain how to get to the answers. I think that it was very amazing that they print sheets for you to work on based on what your doing and I think that was a thing I had never seen before. I think they have amazing teachers and the teachers would sit down with you and explain each question you did not understand. It really helped me improve in math and I think it got me more confident in myself and not wondering if I was right, and instead knowing I am right. It was really great and I think Ms. Chen is a great teacher.

Very appreciative and glad for these wonderful tutors , Mikhail for Emaths , Kai for A maths, Hiero for Chem and especially a loud shout-out for Brian for Pure Bio. These energetic and dedicated tutor really put their heart into their students. My daughter did well and got into a course that she wanted. Thank you very much.

Crucible Education at Dhoby Ghaut is an excellent tuition centre with passionate and dedicated tutors. The lessons are clear, engaging, and tailored to help students truly understand challenging topics. I especially appreciated the supportive learning environment and the 24/7 help via WhatsApp. Highly recommend for students looking to boost their grades and confidence!

hiero is a fantastic tutor at crucible, he is patient and passionate about teaching chemistry and breaks down tough topics and makes it easy to understand. the centre has a great a great learning environment with great tutors⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️

10/10 chem tuition. Lessons are very engaging. Grade improved from failing to a distinction in a year!! Hiero is very approachable and will answer any questions any time u need help. Goated tuition frfr 🗣️🗣️🗣️

Clean and spacious place! Tutors here put in a lot of effort to create a fun community.

Good place to pull up your grades and enjoy learning

place_name: National Library / Lee Kong Chian Reference Library
review_text:
Visited the National Library Building mainly for The Albatross File: Singapore’s Independence Declassified exhibition, and it turned out to be a meaningful experience. The exhibition itself is relatively small, but the historical significance is immense. It provides a fascinating and candid look into the events leading up to Singapore’s separation from Malaysia in 1965, including documents and notes showing that both sides had, by then, come to see separation as the likely path forward. Seeing the actual letters, transcripts, and handwritten notes made this pivotal chapter of Singapore’s history feel very real and personal

I also took some time to roam around the Lee Kong Chian Reference Library and was impressed by how incredibly well managed the entire place is. The building itself is architecturally striking, modern, spacious, and filled with natural light. The library environment feels calm, organized, and conducive for learning and research, with clear signage and well-maintained facilities throughout

place_name: National Library / Lee Kong Chian Reference Library
review_text: The National Library is conveniently located with good traffic access from around the island. It’s a comfy cool place to be if your want to study, research and just reading. You may spent hours but no worries about hunger as the are many choices of food in the area.

place_name: National Library / Lee Kong Chian Reference Library
review_text:
2026 January - Did quick look around, enjoyed reading the stories from the mini exhibition Knots of Time. Looks like there are other exhibitions and all sorts of collection, will be back to explore and read.

Great ambience, nice for researchers and the public alike. Place has plenty of space if you'd like to get some work done, offers brilliant views, and even has a skygarden. Power and water is readily available, and theres even sufficient space for a stroll if you're feeling a mental block coming on.

Will be back to check out the albatross files. The atrium at the bottom is very pleasant, and the vaguely 'heritage' old chang kee seems like a good place to check out as well.

place_name: National Library / Lee Kong Chian Reference Library
review_text: The Albatross File exhibition at NLB is a fascinating showcase of Singapore’s separation history, featuring declassified documents, oral histories, and immersive multimedia displays. It brings to life the tense years leading up to independence, blending archival records with dramatized storytelling.

place_name: National Library / Lee Kong Chian Reference Library
review_text:
Albatross exhibition of Singapore

Thankful for this curated history that bares all that Singaporeans may not know. We had the privilege of having seniors in their 70s who came along, cementing our understanding of what they had gone thru.

The whole setup allowed us to go at our own pace and the voice of our late and retired ministers add a sense of urgency and realism.

A suggestion for those who are not English educated, as the ministers are speaking in English, please allow translator set made avail in various language to our non English speaking seniors.

Lastly the wooden board is super heavy. Made it very difficult for us to lift up the board to read the files.  Lastly is there a need to have the board hidden? Most don’t know it exist until someone lifted it up.

A lovely visit to NLB Central with my family and some youths for the Book Bugs event. The session was engaging and thoughtfully organised, making reading fun and interactive for the children. The space was welcoming and well-maintained, and the staff were friendly and helpful throughout the event.

My kids and the youths we brought along enjoyed themselves and it was meaningful to see them excited about books and storytelling. It’s a great initiative by the library to encourage a love for reading.

The only reason for 4 stars is that the area can get quite crowded during events, but overall it was still a very positive experience. Grateful for the effort put into organising such family-friendly programmes!

Perhaps the largest public library in Singapore. Hard to find a librarian for assistance, and bookings for seats are required if you do not want to be asked to leave just because someone has booked the seats. A really stupid concept for those who would casually come into the library if they are in the area. Throughout the years, NLB has done nothing to enhance the library experience. What the public has been slapped with are rules and regulations which makes visiting the library a chore.

Came purely for the kids section of the library and it sure lived up to our expectations. The little one thoroughly enjoyed himself amidst the aquarium themed library and the endless literature around the area. Very thoughtfully, they also offer floor cushions where you can take and just find a spot to read and chill out on. The hit area with the deck chairs is the central of the kids section but was unfortunately pretty much always filled with people. Great place to spend the weekend letting the kids immerse themselves. Parking is also pretty reasonable at $1.20 per hour on Sundays.

The Central Public Library in Singapore is a vibrant space blending innovation, design, and functionality. Located at the National Library Building, it offers an extensive collection of books, digital resources, and themed spaces catering to all age groups. Highlights include the eco-friendly My Tree House, the world’s first green library for children, and its lush ambiance. The library’s modern facilities, cozy reading nooks, and seamless technology make it an ideal place for study or leisure. Regular workshops and events add to its community spirit. A hub of knowledge and creativity, it’s a must-visit for book lovers and lifelong learners.

Beautiful architecture building.  Lobby has two grand pianos, one is for public performances, another one is for anyone who wants to play!!  3rd floor has a cafe, very quiet and suitable for meetings and discussions.  The rest floors are for books and resources.  An atrium area is large and windy for who doesn’t want strong AC.  Thumbs up!!

This is an excellently located station along the Circle Line, that is even a short walk underground to the Downtown Line. Would have loved if this station were open when I was still a Singapore Management University undergraduate. I still often use this station to pass through the area including my alma mater and many other surrounding places like the Cathedral of the Good Shepherd. Always love the architecture here when taking the escalators within the station. Really clean too! Will be back!

One of the more aesthetically-pleasing train stations in my opinion, with natural light seeping in through transparent glass tops with artificial water features. Creates a really nice ambience within the basement - some spots are even instagram-worthy and great for landscape photography enthusiasts.

It’s typically quiet, except for SMU students who are visiting the university or office workers in the vicinity. It’s a direct sheltered access for them - a great privilege. Signs and directions are well placed and its easy to navigate. You can drop off this station to head to waterloo street, Lazada office, Bencoolen, or the National Museum. There are OCBC and POSB ATMs within the train station as well upon gantry exit, alongside a convenience store. Toilets are available too and are always kept 100% clean. Can’t ask for more to be honest.

This station is the offical MRT station catered for SMU students and surrounding office workers. During the weekend it could be a bit noisy due to the vicinity being the practise ground for many SMU dance groups and other clubs. There is also a seven eleven store located right outside MRT gantry

Bras Basah MRT Station was extensively deep, and I liked the architectural design. There weren't many people when I arrived at this MRT station.

There's quite the number of bus stops located near several exits of the MRT station, which was quite accessible. The toilet is, as per usual, clean. Wouldn't be surprised actually at this point. Overall, quite a neat underground MRT station.

This is a very nice and very iconic station because I got to walk here as my first station when I was in Singapore!!!

The trains were very on time, the ambience is cool, the people are extremely orderly and walks very fast. But the place is not crowded and is a shining example of what a metropolis should be.

First experience with the MRT in Singapore. Once we finally found the entrance it was quite easy to navigate. Many small restraunts in this Bugis station, even a 7-11 where I got a SLURPEE. Loved using the MRT. Very clean and safe.

Singapore’s MRT system is clean, efficient, and extremely reliable. Trains arrive frequently, stations are well maintained, and signage is clear, making it easy for both locals and tourists to navigate the city.

The network covers most parts of Singapore, with convenient connections between lines and seamless transfers to buses. Stations are generally equipped with lifts, escalators, and clear announcements, making travel accessible and comfortable.

Overall, the MRT is one of the best public transport systems in the world—fast, affordable, and stress-free. It’s the easiest way to get around Singapore.

Bugis MRT station is very different now it has becomes the interchange for the Downtown Line.

It also have many more facilities and better connected.

Crowded, but serves its purpose well.

One of the key public transport or mrt station in Singapore connecting travellers to bugis area having lots of malls and restaurants. It’s a happening place. The transport route linked this station to important hubs like the airport and the downtown, as well to many other tourist attraction areas. One thing I suggest to improve is to make the connectivity network more complete by having a covered pedestrian to the nearby queen street bus terminal. This will really make this mrt station get connected well with the bus networks including the crossborder routes.

Bugis MRT Station (DT14/EW12) Review

Located in the heart of Singapore’s vibrant Bugis district, Bugis MRT is a key interchange station on the Downtown (DTL) and East-West (EWL) Lines. It provides seamless connectivity to business hubs, shopping malls, and cultural landmarks like Bugis Junction, Bugis Street, and Kampong Glam.

The station is well-maintained, clean, and efficiently managed, with clear signages for easy navigation. Peak-hour congestion is common, especially at the interchange area, but crowd flow is generally smooth. Escalators, lifts, and tactile flooring ensure accessibility for all passengers, including the elderly and those with mobility needs.

Facilities include convenience stores, ATMs, and ticketing machines, while exits lead directly to major malls, office buildings, and bus stops for further connectivity. Overall, Bugis MRT is a modern, convenient, and strategic transit hub in central Singapore.
SBS and NUS shuttle service available at the stop.

place_name: Aft Bras Basah Stn Exit A
review_text: Goog

place_name: Aft Bras Basah Stn Exit A
review_text:
I would like to share my feedback regarding my recent experience with your customer service for ntuc income

I only wanted to find out two very simple pieces of information:

1. Which credit card is currently linked to my policy for the annual premium deduction.
2. The exact date when the annual deduction will be made.

Since this deduction only happens once a year, it is natural for customers to forget these details. I called your hotline expecting a quick and simple answer, but the experience became unnecessarily complicated.

First, it was very difficult to reach the correct department. I was transferred multiple times, and each transfer required me to wait on hold for at least five minutes.

Secondly, I was given incorrect instructions. One staff member directed me to follow a sequence of telephone menu options (such as pressing 1, then 4, then 2), but the information was inaccurate and did not connect me to the correct department.

After finally reaching another staff member, I was informed that my enquiry was still outside their scope and that I would need to be transferred yet again. By then, I had already spent nearly 30 minutes on the phone. I therefore requested that, instead of transferring me again, someone simply return my call.

About an hour later, Doris called me. She told me will sent me messsage then i need to click to login and then asked me to log in to my email and told me that I should have received an email on 20 May containing a letter with the details of my registered credit card for the premium deduction.

I checked my inbox while on the call and found that I had not received any email on 20 May. After searching further, I located an email dated 21 May instead. Doris insisted that the required information was contained in a letter attached to that email. However, after spending several minutes searching together, we could not find any such letter or attachment. This suggested that she was not familiar with the email or document that had actually been sent to customers.

She then asked me to log in to the me income app to check . Only then did she guide me to the correct section where my policy letters were stored. Within just two minutes, I was able to view all the information I needed, including the registered credit card and the scheduled premium deduction date.

This made me wonder why this was not the very first instruction given to me. If your staff had understood my enquiry from the beginning, they could simply have said:

“Please log in to the e-Insurance portal using your Singpass, go to the Policy Letters section, and you will be able to view your registered credit card details and scheduled premium deduction date.”

This approach would not have required me many stepss, email address or other personal information over the phone. It would also have resolved my enquiry within two minutes.

Instead, a simple enquiry that should have taken only a couple of minutes ended up taking almost one hour due to multiple transfers, incorrect information, unnecessary verification and confusion about where the information was actually located.

I hope you will review your customer service process, improve staff training, reduce unnecessary call transfers, and ensure that your staff know exactly where customers can access basic policy information online. This would greatly improve the customer experience while saving valuable time for both your customers and your staff.

place_name: Aft Bras Basah Stn Exit A
Aft Bras Basah Stn Exit A
review_text: The bus stop is right outside the BrasBash station exit, has a roof and benches, and many different types of buses stop there.

place_name: Aft Bras Basah Stn Exit A

review_text:
Situated right next to the bustling Bugis Street and the famous Kwan Im Thong Hood Cho Temple, Albert Centre is a must-explore hawker complex.

Chwee Kueh are little steamed rice cakes, light and smooth with preserved radish) on top. It makes it salty, sweet, and bursting with umami. Adding a bit of the sambal chili gives it a slow-burn kick that makes it addictive.

Kueh Pie Tee is a delightful Peranakan snack. The pastry shells are perfectly crisp, holding a juicy filling of shredded turnip. It’s a wonderful contrast of textures in a single bite, crunchy on the outside and savory-sweet on the inside.

Bak Chor Mee is Minced Meat Noodles. This bowl has a fantastic balance of vinegar and chili. The noodles are cooked al dente, and the variety of textures, from the soft minced meat to the earthy liver and savory braised mushrooms, make every bite interesting.

Hainanese Chicken Rice is my favorite. The chicken is incredibly silky and tender, paired with rice that is fragrant and perfectly oily without being greasy. The side of bok choy adds a nice crunch to the meal😍

Albert Centre Market & Food Centre is quite popular.  This food center is surrounded by Sim Lim Square, famous Chinese Temple and Indian Temple and many retail shops and office buildings.  This market is quite packed most of the time especially during office lunch time.  You can also see many tourists here.  There are many nice food here such as Fish noddle, Chicken Rice, Lor Mee, Dessert, Muslim and Indian stalls.

A very authentic local hawker centre right in the heart of Bugis. Plenty of food choices at affordable prices, from fishball noodles to yong tau foo and traditional desserts.
What I like most is the old-school atmosphere — not fancy, but full of character and local flavour. Some stalls have long queues, which is usually a good sign.😋
It can get crowded during peak hours, people & birds 🤭 but overall a great place to enjoy real Singapore food. Definitely worth a visit if you’re in the area.

Hope they put no entry to bird with nets charge.🤭

Pro tip: come during lunch time and see where the locals and office workers line up. They know where the good food is at!

INCREDIBLE Chwee Kueh. The store is named 水糕 if I remember correctly and it's all they sell. Super melt in your mouth, and you order how many pieces you want (minimum is 2). Super affordable as well.

Popiah next stall over was not bad too.

Third is a pictured local favorite dessert stall. It almost always has a line and only sells 4 dessert items. I got the Tau Suan (it's hot) and it was nutty, soup was silky, and not too sweet. Would HIGHLY recommend getting a dessert soup here!

All the stalls pictured after are on my bucket list as each had long lines during the lunch rush 👀

Would definitely come back the next time I visit Singapore!

​My first visit back to Albert Food Centre since the renovation and it looks impressive! It's bright, airy, and much more comfortable thanks to the improved ventilation.
I tried the Signature Noodle & Soup ($6.90), and the Soon Kueh ($1.40 for 2) today. Overall, there’s a fantastic range of food available at this food centre so come and try it out!

Decent spot for variety, but not for those craving familiar Pakistani flavours
Visited Lau Pa Sat (Lay Pa Sat) recently in Singapore. It’s a lively and iconic hawker centre with plenty of options and a nice atmosphere, especially in the evening when the streets are closed off for dining.
We tried the halal stall and ordered chicken rice noodles, chicken basil vegetables, and tea tree something. While the portions were generous and the service was quick, the overall taste was quite different from what we expected. Everything had a strong seafood-like flavour profile (even the non-seafood items), which didn’t really appeal to us as I can’t stand sea food smell. It felt quite far from Pakistani taste preferences – the spices, seasoning, and balance were very different.
If you’re in the mood to explore local Singaporean or Southeast Asian hawker food and try new things, Lau Pa Sat is a great place with lots of variety. However, if you’re particular about your food and want something closer to Pakistani cuisine, this might not be the best choice.
Cleanliness was just fine for a busy hawker centre, and the pricing was reasonable. Good for tourists or casual explorers, but we probably won’t rush back.

fantastic place to grab some food and/or beer. Famous for the skewers on the street, especially the chicken satay. Plenty of options to choose from, Indian, Arabic, Chinese, Singaporean, etc. There's something for everyone. Good ventilation and plenty of tables but the place is always packed and noisy (it doesn't take away the enjoyment). A must to visit.

There's no need to write much about this place; you simply must stop in for a fantastic meal. There are plenty of street food joints offering a variety of Asian cuisines. Everything is perfectly seasoned, with plenty to choose from, so everyone should easily find something to their liking. And the prices are very affordable by Singaporean standards.

Lau Pa Sat: A must-visit for the lively night vibe!

I visited at 9 PM on a Friday, and it was absolutely crowded! The energy outside on Satay Street was incredible, with everyone enjoying grilled food in the open air. Inside the historic building, there are plenty of food stalls to choose from.

The Drinks:
One thing to watch out for is that there are very few drink stalls, so the lines are very long. I bought an Iced Mango Peach ($2.80), but I wouldn't recommend it. It was extremely sweet and didn't contain any fresh fruit—it tasted mostly like syrup. I suggest skipping the "juices" here if you want something fresh.

The Food & Service:
I tried the Thunder Tea Rice ($6.20). It was okay, though it didn't quite fit my personal taste. After eating, the service is efficient; you can either clear your own tray or wait for the staff who come by frequently to collect them.

The Verdict:
It is very crowded and noisy with loud music, but it’s a "must-visit" to experience the local Singaporean vibe. Just be prepared for the crowds!

Tried Na Na Curry at Lau Pa Sat because saw that they are awarded Michelin Bib Gourmand 10 years in a row!

Their chicken curry is rich, slightly creamy, and super comforting. The spices are well-balanced, not too heavy but still very satisfying.

The chicken is tender and soaks up the curry nicely, making every bite flavourful. Pairs really well with both the rice and roti prata!

place_name: Shifu Food Court - Heritage of Flavours
review_text: A vibrant, heritage-themed food court with both air-conditioned & outdoor seating. Offers a good mix of local & international options from about 14 stalls. Portions are ok & prices slightly steep, but expected for the city area. Some stalls open 24 hours, making it a rare late-night makan spot. A great place for a casual bite after browsing the complex’s bookstores & art spaces.

place_name: Shifu Food Court - Heritage of Flavours
review_text:
Shifu Food Court has recently reopened on 9th October 2024 in the Bras Basah Complex, revitalizing over two decades of culinary tradition since 2001 with a stunning S$350,000 renovation. This vibrant 498㎡ space accommodates up to 430 guests and is open 24/7, making it an ideal spot for night owls seeking late-night bites.

The alfresco area evokes the charm of classic street stalls, featuring colorful furnishings, tropical planters, and fairy lights. Inside, heritage graphic art light boxes and LED neon signage create a nostalgic atmosphere, complemented by unique display shelves inspired by shophouse windows.

Shifu boasts 14 stalls featuring the diversity of Singaporean flavors. Ah Gong KKM offers creative fusion instant noodles, including a must-try Spicy Prawn variant. The aptly named Thai Chef serves a delectable Pad Thai, while Okinawa Ramen is known for its signature tonkotsu broth and dishes like Karaage Soba. Chun Noodle Bar serves potentially the best wanton noodles, such as the Truffle Char Siu with Wanton Noodle (Dry) and Crispy Wanton with Truffle Mayo. Haru Haru Japanese presents flavorful Mentai donburi bowls with juicy pork, crispy chicken katsu, or flavorful chicken karaage. 👍🏻😋😋

For coffee lovers, Shifu Café, Heritage of Flavours, offers barista-style coffee for just S$2, making it a perfect pitstop for nearby office workers. Don’t miss Penang Island's authentic Malaysian street food, especially the indulgent Penang Cendol with Durian. 😍😍😍

Additionally, Shifu collaborates with students from the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts to feature murals of local landmarks, transforming the food court into an artistic canvas. To celebrate its grand launch, Shifu will offer promotions and 20% discounts for students, with free Wifi creating an inviting space for relaxation and study.

Owner Richard Lee shares, “It’s a place where the old meets the new.” Stay tuned to Shifu’s social media for updates and experience this vibrant culinary hub—a unique celebration of heritage, flavors, and creativity! ❤️❤️

place_name: Shifu Food Court - Heritage of Flavours
review_text:
I visited this new outlet early one morning to avoid the reported crowds that swarmed this place as it is newly opened.
There were only a handful of morning patrons who were there for breakfast. My fave stall that sold soup kway teow was no longer there but was replaced by the bak chor mee stall.
I tried the pork porridge and found it tasty.
For $4.50, there were about a few good slices of pork and tasted not salty which I liked.

Maybe next time I will try the other stalls. Try walking to the 2 ends of the food court to see what's available.

The birds were a nuisance still. The lights in the non-airconditioned seating area did  not deter them. They seemed very conditioned as to how to fly in and around.

place_name: Shifu Food Court - Heritage of Flavours
review_text:
Tried the Japanese karaage and katsu Don. The chicken is my preferred... the sauce is really good. Sinfully delicious crispy chicken skin. My friend tried unagi and said it tastes great too.

The roti prata is good as well,  congee is good,  and the groupa fish soup tastes good although a bit salty.

After the renovation, it's very well ventilated with a good selection of foods. A bit pricey but expected in the city area...

The coffee could be nicer with a different bean.

place_name: Shifu Food Court - Heritage of Flavours
review_text:
I had the traditional Laksa from one of the stalls at the food court. The name is “Ding Ji” stall. The aunt who makes the noodles very very rude. I paid with 10 note and the price is 5 dollars. The lady said she already gave me the changes but she didn’t. So I ask her do you want to check with the cctv? So then she rudely gave me the 5 dollars back. Then I do need the receipt because of business travel declaration. She didn’t not want to give me and she turned back and keep cleaning the dishes. But I keep waiting and said I want the receipt. She was yelling when gave me the receipt and said “GO”

So that’s my experience the first day in Singapore.

An under-rated food court that has a good mix of local and other hawker food at reasonable prices.

I recommend Haru Haru stalls, one that sells mentaiko katsu don, while the other sells ramen with no pork...

The wanton noodle stall looks good but I have yet to try.

If you are near the vicinity, at Central Library, Bras Basah or Bugis, can try the food here.

I had the chicken curry noodles from the chicken rice stall. Worst I've ever eaten. Bowl of noodles came soaked with oil. My tau pok was all soaked with oil. I had to scoop it out manually and the oil filled the small bowl! At least I thought the chicken would be the saving grace but alas. All I got was a miserably small chopped up chicken wing. So you can imagine it is mostly bones and skin. Costs 6.50 but I'll not eat it if it's free.

In early October 2024

Today was my first visit since the revamp.

The food court looks so much better and there is a much better variety of stalls compared to the past.

I ordered for from Thai Chef staff. The service was quick and efficient, and the basil pork was excellent as was the sunny side up egg. I will definitely be back to try more dishes from this stall and food from the other stalls

I ordered the minced pork with basil leaves from Thai Chef stall
.

17 Nov 2024 - this is my 3rd time that I bought the same dish from Thai Chef.

On the first occasion, the basil pork rice was done nicely. Please refer to the last 2 photos as they speak for themself. There is no consistency in their food!

On the second visit 2 weeks later, it wasn't as good.

Today 17 Nov 2024, the same dish was poorly made. The basil.pork was dry and the sunny side up egg was way overcooked. The saving grace was the soup and fried popiah. This will probably be my last time ordering food from this stall.

Overall the food court is nice and comfortable and a good place to visit and try out the other food.

24hours foodcourt with a wide range of cuisines at Bras Basah Complex. It's air conditioned but some tables are cooler than others, so pick wisely.

The Mentai Pork Katsu Curry ($10.90) is from Haru Haru. Katsu was crisp and pretty juicy, and curry was rich and thick, leaning towards the tangy flavours of tomatoes. However, we thought the mentai sauce did not complement the curry well, and also would be better if it was torched.

Wanton Mee ($5.50) is from Chun Noodle Bar, and this was pretty good! Noodles were perfectly QQ, and the sauce was yummy too. For the regular size, the portion of the thick charsiu was sizeable, and they even give 2 pieces of fried wanton.

Lastly, Red Bean Chendol ($3.50) is from Penang Island was decent, lacking in the pandan fragrance and gula melaka taste.

Visited this little gem after the renovation not too long ago, and whilst it still feels cramped (the aisles between tables are so narrow it’s one-way walking only), the ventilation is better and looks quite cheerful. There’s also outdoor seating around the corners which is a big help during peak hours. Oh yes there’s also free wifi.

My first time here and was overwhelmed by choice of food stalls - the smells entering the food court were so good, it was hard to decide which stall to patronize. Bro got pork tonkatsu on rice & I tried dry beef brisket & tripe, also a plate of har cheong ;prawn rice rolls) to share.

After a short wait, the buzzers had us collecting our orders (btw tray return station faces the yong tau fu stall or on your right if you enter from north bridge rd side of complex). The pork was thick cut coated in a very crispy crunchy batter, sprinkled with bonito & seaweed flakes.

You must dollop a generous scoop of their chili sauce to accompany the beef noodles, it is so good and makes the QQ noodles taste even better. The brisket melts in your mouth and the tripe had a nice chewy texture to it. The bowl of soup was tasty too. The har cheong was a little eggy, but it still tasted very good.

My first time seeing a can of OMG in the chiller section of drinks stall. Oh My Gut is an alcoholic pro biotic beer! Only dessert here was the Penang chendol from Penang Island stall. A good lunch indeed.

Went on a weekday around 2-plus and the place was already quite packed. It’s a small cozy spot to enjoy coffee and cakes.

They were out of brownies when I arrived, but the server kindly offered to bake a fresh one since I mentioned I came especially for it. Best decision ever—the brownie was amazing! Moist, chocolaty, not overly sweet, with a touch of chocolate lava inside. Honestly one of the best brownies I’ve had, and definitely worth coming back for.

The carrot cake was also delightful—light, moist, and generously portioned. Paired really well with the brownie.
Nov 2025
I ordered a mocha, which was balanced and not overly sweet or chocolaty, though not quite to my taste. Next time, I’d like to try their white coffee or dirty coffee instead.

I can’t wait to return for more brownies! At only $5 nett per slice, their cakes are a real steal.

Feb 2026
Tried their matcha latte. Pretty good. Not overly sweet.
Brownie still the best. 😍

The BEST Dirty Latte I’ve ever had in my life! Seriously, the flavor is on another level. ☕️✨
The cafe itself is quite cozy with limited seating, but the quality and taste of the coffee speak for themselves. If you’re a true coffee enthusiast, this spot is a must-visit.

Only came twice but amazed twice. Good coffee selections and good brewing skill to bring out the coffee profiles. Currently my top favourite coffee place. Limited seats though, it was quite full during my both visits. Outdoor seating is hot.

We had a hojicha latte with oat milk (without sugar) and a dirty latte + a fruit crumble cake. The hojicha latte was amazing! Totally recommended! Coffee was a little acidic for my taste. Would not recommend the fruit crumble cake. The texture is too chewy for my liking. nonetheless, it’s a nice place to chill and have a cup of coffee for a hot day.

One of the most complete cafe I’m looking for, a place that serves good quality filter coffee, a good Dirty, and at a affordable price.
Their Dirty Coffee is similar to what I’ve tried in Thailand, the contrast between hot espresso over chilled milk mixture is pronounced.
Filtered coffee is pretty good as well, clean, bright, tea-like texture, and pretty aromatic (Raisins, Walnut, Pecan etc). But, I will be much appreciated if they use a sensory cup for their filtered coffee.

place_name: Twenty Eight Cafe+ Kopi 1930 National Gallery
review_text:
Place gets crowded and you choose your own table and order from the QR code. Food is priced on the higher side - but taste wise doesn’t really match the price. It’s around 20-30 for the western dishes and about 10-15 for the kopitiam local dishes.

Seafood aglio olio 3/5 :

Generous amount of lala clams and prawns, and seafood was very fresh. Aglio olio wise could have been more flavourful for the pasta and was a little soggy.

Kaya soufflé pancake 2/5 :

Delicious homemade kaya and crispy bread crackers on the side, as well as delicious salted cream and coconut flakes on the pancake, just that the pancake was not jiggly enough and too solid, resulting in a pretty cakey mouthfeel and texture instead of a melt in your mouth feeling.

Pesto pasta:

Creamy, sauce coated the mafalde pasta well. Could use a little more pesto flavour imo!

Ayam berempah thigh 2/5:

Could have been more flavourful, strips cut too thin so they taste dry and stringy when fried

Tater tots w mentaiko sauce 2/5:

Could have given more mentaiko sauce for the amount of taters given, taters has a slightly over oily taste (I get that it’s deep fried but it was a little too gelat still)

Bee Hoon with luncheon meat and Ngoh Hiang 3/5 :

Was $9, a little pricey.

Ribena fizz 3/5:

$5, tastes like it sounds.

place_name: Twenty Eight Cafe+ Kopi 1930 National Gallery
review_text:
🟨 Twenty Eight Cafe  ( 🩷 new)
1 Saint Andrew's Rd, #01-17
National Gallery, Singapore 178957

🟨 Location
***************
🟨 Visited this newly-opened cafe at The National Gallery with a friend. 🟨 The cafe is located on ground floor.

🟨 Once you catch sight of the museum gift shop ➖️ you turn to the right, and the cafe is there.
_____________________________________
🔵 Ambience
*****************
🔵 The cafe has 2 dining concepts. 🔵 One side of the cafe is occupied by Kopi 1930, which serves local Singapore dishes and local beverages.

🔵  The 2nd portion of the space features "Twenty Eight" cafe ➖️ which is a western style concept ( ➡️ serving western style brunch items and such).

🟥 For these 2 cafe concepts, no pork and no lard is used in all their dishes.

🔵  Adjacent to the long glass counter are more seats available. ●Over at this end, the cafe space stretches over a long dining hall area. ▪︎The interior decor palette is a soothing mix of white, black, grey, cream, brown and dark green hues.

🔵 There are window nook seats on one side of the cafe. ● These cosy window box seats offer a quiet sense of privacy, with natural day-light streaming in through the large vintage style latticed windows.
_____________________________________

🟢 Food
**************
🟢 My friend and I ordered 4 items:-
***************************************
🟩  2 Mugs of Teh-C (➖️ ie local tea with Carnation Milk priced at SGD3.20 per cup).

🟩 Kaya & Butter on Crackers / SGD2.50 for 2 pieces.

🟢 Chicken Thigh Berempah / SGD8.90 ( side dish)

🟢 All items were well crafted. The Teh-C was rich with the creaminess of the evaporated milk and had the depth and flavour of well brewed local tea notes.

🟢 I love the Chicken Thigh Berempah dish. The chicken pieces were well-marinated with local spices and were served hot. They were crispy, succulent and featured unami flavours.

🟢 The Kaya & Butter Crackers brought a sense of nostalgia to me. Because this lovely heritage snack is what I used to eat during my childhood days in the 1970s.
_______________________________________
🟪  Service = The staff at the order station ( cashier's counter) were all friendly, helpful and affable. I also met Ms Cynthia Lim, the owner of the cafe.

🟪 She had a most pleasant and kind demeanour and was amiable and very nice to talk to. We had a short but interesting chat about the new cafe's star dish items.

🟪 I was informed by Ms Cynthia that their other 2 outlets at Wilkie Road area ( ➖️ also named Kopi 1930 & Twenty Eight cafe) features different signature star dishes.
________________________________________
✳️ Overall, this newly-opened cafe is a lovely spot to take a short break.  ● Am impressed with the level of service here and I wish to thank Ms Cynthia and her crew members for their omotenashi spirit of hospitality.

❇️ Monday / 3 Nov 2025

place_name: Twenty Eight Cafe+ Kopi 1930 National Gallery
review_text: I really enjoyed the kopitiam-style dishes here the flavors were authentic and satisfying. The ambiance, especially with the beautiful setting alongside the National Gallery Singapore, made the experience even more special. The service was attentive and pleasant as well. It was my first visit, and it definitely exceeded expectations ,well worth it!

place_name: Twenty Eight Cafe+ Kopi 1930 National Gallery
review_text: We had a lovely lunch here with my child. The food was delicious, and the wait time was pleasantly short. Service was excellent, with attentive and friendly staff throughout. The atmosphere was bright and welcoming on a sunny afternoon, and our window seats overlooking the Padang made the experience even more special.

place_name: Twenty Eight Cafe+ Kopi 1930 National Gallery
review_text: Very cosy place at the National Gallery to have a relaxing meal. Toast sets were OK.

Had a very enjoyable evening here with a nice beer and food pairing. The Sapporo beers were cold, refreshing and perfect for sharing, especially with the crispy fried chicken. The chicken was served hot with a very crunchy coating, nicely seasoned with a spicy powder that gave every bite an extra kick. The meat inside was still juicy, and the fried basil added a nice fragrance.

The edamame was simple but fresh, making it a good light snack between drinks. I also liked the cosy atmosphere of the place, with comfortable seating, warm lighting and a nice bar setting that makes it suitable for a casual night out with friends. Overall, good food, good drinks and a relaxing vibe. Definitely worth coming back again.

A nice place for a relaxed evening with a lively atmosphere, especially suitable for group gatherings and celebrations.

➡️ Food selection is quite wide, though the overall quality is average. However, the variety makes it easy for groups with different preferences. A recommended choice is their classic combo, which includes oyster mee sua, braised pork rice, and Taiwanese fried chicken. The drinks menu is extensive, with happy hour deals starting from around $12. Service is efficient, with a call bell system at each table so staff can respond quickly when needed.

➡️ On top of this, there is live band performance at every single night, creating a vibrant and energetic ambiance.

➡️ For birthdays, pre-booked celebration tables come with thoughtful touches such as a free cake, Polaroid photo, props, and a birthday song with singer blessing, adding a nice personal touch. A friendly reminder that the band also traditionally invites the birthday celebrant (especially first-time visitors) on stage to sing a song :)

A great place to wind down after a long day at work. The food quality is average, but there’s a wide variety of options to choose from. The alcohol selection is also extensive, and happy hour deals start from around $12.

Service is efficient — there’s a call bell on the table, so staff respond quickly whenever you need assistance. When I visited on a Friday night, there was a live band performing. It wasn’t too loud, especially if you sit in the outdoor area, where you can enjoy the music while still having enough space for a comfortable conversation.

Overall, a nice spot for a relaxed evening with good vibes.

a3rd time here for food. was telling my friends the food here is pretty good.
So we ordered a few items here as shown in the picture.
The 蛋饼, 盐酥鸡, 卤肉饭 is good.
but the mentaiko sweet potato is very very disappointing.
it 100% does not taste like mentaiko
it just taste like mayo.
i feedback to the staff saying that it tastes not like mentaiko.
but what they replied me was that it looks like the picture as shown on the menu.
so what (i think) they are trying to infer is that as long as the food looks like the picture, it doesnt matter whether the food taste like what the NAME suggest.
i told the staff that i dont want it.
And the manager (probably) came back to my table and said that I cannot order food and reject it because it does not taste like what it should taste like, what if i taste all the food and reject all the food?
i think this comment is uncalled for.
they did cancel the mentaiko fries for me after i told him off.
overall the service crew is helpful.
but unfortunately i have to give it a 1 star for overall and service because if this manager.

I came here twice - first time at night, the second time in the evening before the sky darkens.

The food is flavourful, but a bit salty for my preference. I love the drinks, whether it's a cocktail or mocktail... they did well in this department.

At night, they've a live band. I recommend to sit further away from the stage, as it can be quite loud and it's hard to have a good conversation with one another.

Most of the time, we'd have to wait for the singer to finish singing to speak, if not, we will be shouting above the music. 😅

We came here for dinner to celebrate our wedding anniversary, but unfortunately the experience was disappointing. The first dish we ordered, grilled broccolini, was dry and bland—I had to request olive oil and salt to be added. The next dish, the half grilled lobster, was even worse. While my husband was removing the flesh, he realized it was not fully cooked.

The young lady serving us said they would prepare a new one, but when the dish was returned, it appeared to be the same piece. We wouldn’t have minded as long as it was properly cooked, but the situation was handled poorly.

Overall, the staff seemed disengaged and unenthusiastic, often standing around looking bored. The only exception was Sebastian, a new staff member who had only been working there for two days—he was warm and friendly.

Every dish was served plainly, with no sides or thoughtful plating, which did not justify the high price point. There was also no service recovery or acknowledgment of our anniversary.

Sadly, we won’t be returning.

A lovely spot to unwind after a long day. The ambience is stylish yet relaxed, made all the more enjoyable with live music that sets a warm, easy-going mood without being overpowering. Drinks selection is great, and the overall atmosphere is perfect for a casual evening of conversation and quiet indulgence. A very pleasant place to chill and linger.

Tried the following dishes: • Starters
- Oysters: Ponzu vinaigrette, ikura garlic, preserved black bean
- Crab Salad: Granny Smith, crème fraîche, ikura, papadum •
Mains
- Braised Wagyu Beef Cheek: Sweet pea, pomme purée, mushrooms, natural jus
- Grilled Pork Belly: Celeriac purée, pineapple salsa, scallions
•
The beef cheek is beautifully executed—fork-tender and balanced by the sweetness of the peas and the silky purée.
•
The pork belly is nicely grilled with a gentle char, and the meat stays moist. • Sweets
- Basque Cheesecake: Fresh berries
•
Cocktails
- Pink Floye: Vodka, rose, evaporated milk
- Reverie Sling: Gin, mixed fruits citrus, soda
•
@cheechin.chew on Instagram

Tasty and underrated food. The Thunder fries is a must order. We had 3 servings of it because it was so addictive. Attentive, kind and pleasant service. Boss of the restaurant was very friendly and warm. This place serves good food and relaxed ambience.

Extremely disappointing, grossly overpriced, and poorly executed.

We dined as a group of 4 and ordered the Chef’s Special Menu (6 courses at $128 per person). What was served was completely unacceptable for a menu at this price.

The 1st to 4th courses were laughably small, quail-egg–sized tapas that vanished in a single bite. The main course striploin was served in just three tiny slices, cold, dry, and not cooked to any discernible doneness. It failed on every basic level.

When we shared our disappointment with the manager, his response was dismissive and lacking empathy, with our feedback largely brushed aside rather than addressed.

We were still hungry and had to order additional à la carte mains. Even then, the pork belly was nowhere close to Burnt Ends standards, lacking proper grilling, depth of flavour, and finishing. If the restaurant is positioning itself around an ex-Burnt Ends chef team, this comparison does not hold up at all.

Overall, the Chef’s Special Menu is massively overpriced, poorly portioned, and badly executed. This felt like style over substance and misleading positioning.
Would not return. Strongly do not recommend.

Saw this cafe in the Bras Basah complex and decided to get a drink to cool down. The drinks were good. Their food menu looks interesting and had a decent variety that looked worth a try, maybe another time. Staff are friendly but service is minimal as you order by the machine like many places these days.

Great food and even better service! The menu is well-curated with plenty of choices. The Mentaiko Chicken Katsu Burger was spot on—super crispy with a sauce that hits all the right notes. If you're a pasta lover, the Mentaiko Pasta is a must-try for its rich, creamy texture.

​I really appreciated that the Blue Pea and Matcha drinks weren't too sugary, focusing instead on deep, authentic flavors. For dessert, definitely go for the Chrysanthemum Goji ice cream if you want something refreshing, though the Oolong Peach is also a fantastic blend.

​Shoutout to the amazing staff for being so welcoming and helpful. Highly recommended!

We came for the viral sheep matcha, which was cute and delicious! The harvest pasta was nice too. However, there was this waitress with long dyed hair in a ponytail who had an attitude and looked at us as if we owed her money. Otherwise, would come back for the matcha and pasta.

Really really nice tarts, and I enjoyed the matcha sheep drink. The strawberry matcha is meh due to the fakeness of the strawberry puree.

I love love love the decoration and vibe of the place, and the service is really gd.

Took a Star off because they didn’t on the aircon! It’s sooo warm!!

Came here for lunch on a weekday. Ordered the Steak & Eggs cos my friend wanted to eat steak. I addes the Sourdough bread.

The medium raw steak was cooked with the right tenderness and I love how crunchy the sourdough bread was, best paired with the sunny-side eggs.

Price is affordable. Good place for brunch. They closed early before dinner.

Wunderfolks is famous for their pastries. I didn't get a chance to try this time.

There is also a 15% when you pay via Grab Pay.

Visited this place ever first time and was surprised to see it totally quiet place as compared to the last time hype. It was such a famous town place in the past and now it has only art galleries, photo and printing shops, music and dance schools, some exam preparation schools, one bookstore, art friend and other small shops at different floors. Iconic place of Singapore 🇸🇬 and it is still maintained well and kept clean. Some pics of the place and vicinity.

Bras basah Complex is one of the oldest malls we have been going since young.
